changes.full_summary_block:
### Enrichment from Commentary on the Second Degree
Source section: Commentary on the Second Degree

This document is a commentary on the Fellowcraft (Second Degree) ceremony, authored by VWBro Rev Neville Barker Cryer. It is structured to provide explanations during fifteen distinct pauses within the ritual, clarifying the events that have just occurred.
practical_elements: Commentary delivered during the ritual
Fifteen pauses during the ceremony
Explanation provided after each pause
source_notes: Commentary on the Second Degree | Commentary on the Second Degree | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\commentary_on_the_second_Degree.md#commentary-on-the-second-degree | section 1

changes.full_summary_block:
### Enrichment from Commentary on the Second Degree
Source section: The Apprentice's Progress and Readiness for Passing

This section discusses the historical requirements for an Apprentice to progress to a Fellow Craft in operative times, which included seven years of indentured service and specific tasks. It contrasts this with modern speculative Freemasonry, questioning whether current apprentices truly understand the Craft beyond memorization, and highlights the concern of automatic advancement. It also notes the practice in some European lodges where candidates must write about their learning before advancing.
practical_elements: Seven years of indentured service (operative times)
Completion of special tasks and reaching a standard (operative times)
Writing an essay on learned Masonic principles (some European lodges)
changes.candidate_lesson: A candidate's progress should be marked by genuine understanding of the Craft, not just memorization. Advancement should not be automatic but based on demonstrated learning and readiness.
tradition_notes: In operative times, an Apprentice's progress was marked by seven years of indentured service, special tasks, and reaching a specific standard.
Some European lodges require candidates to write about their Masonic learning before advancing to the next degree.
caution_notes: Beware of automatic advancement without genuine understanding of the Craft.
Ensure apprentices learn and understand the Craft beyond mere memorization of answers.
source_notes: Commentary on the Second Degree | The Apprentice's Progress and Readiness for Passing | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\commentary_on_the_second_Degree.md#the-apprentice-s-progress-and-readiness-for-passing | section 2

changes.full_summary_block:
### Enrichment from Commentary on the Second Degree
Source section: Divine Aid and the Square at the Entrance

To attain the privileges of the Second Degree, divine aid is necessary, as human effort alone is insufficient. This spiritual lesson is a pervasive theme in English Masonry. The Square, presented at the entrance, symbolizes the need for both manual and mental skill, expected of a fully trained apprentice, and serves as a reminder to act 'on the square' with all humanity.
practical_elements: The Square is presented to the candidate's breast at the entrance to the Second Degree.
changes.symbolic_meaning: Divine aid symbolizes the necessity of God's help for spiritual advancement and the attainment of Masonic privileges. The Square symbolizes the need for skill (manual and mental) and the moral imperative to act with integrity and fairness ('on the square') towards all people.
changes.candidate_lesson: Candidates are taught that their own efforts are insufficient for true progress in the Second Degree and that divine assistance is essential. They are also reminded of the importance of developing both practical and intellectual skills, and of conducting themselves with honesty and integrity in all interactions.
tradition_notes: The spiritual lesson of needing God's help pervades all English Masonry.
source_notes: Commentary on the Second Degree | Divine Aid and the Square at the Entrance | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\commentary_on_the_second_Degree.md#divine-aid-and-the-square-at-the-entrance | section 3

changes.full_summary_block:
### Enrichment from Commentary on the Second Degree
Source section: Passing the Junior Warden: The Gate of the Lodge

This section details the historical and symbolic significance of passing the Junior Warden in the Fellow Craft degree. It explains that the practice originates from 17th-century Guild Masonry, where Masons were questioned for passwords before a lodge could open in a new degree. In early Moderns lodges, Wardens guarded the 'gate' of the lodge, each associated with a pillar. The Junior Warden's word of passing grants the candidate permission to enter his part of the lodge gate, with full access to a Lodge of Fellows granted after passing the second pillar.
practical_elements: Every individual Mason had to be questioned for passwords before a lodge could be opened in a new degree in 17th-century Guild Masonry and Irish working.
The Junior Warden gives the word of passing.
The candidate will pass the second pillar to gain full access to a Lodge of Fellows.
changes.symbolic_meaning: The Junior Warden, by guarding the 'gate' and giving the word of passing, symbolizes the initial entry point and the structured process of gaining access to the Fellow Craft degree. The two pillars, originally guarding the lodge entrance, represent distinct stages of advancement and the historical requirement for Masons to be vetted before progressing.
changes.candidate_lesson: The candidate learns that progression within Freemasonry is a structured and authorized process, requiring verification and permission at each stage, symbolized by passing the Wardens and their associated pillars.
tradition_notes: In 17th-century Guild Masonry and current Irish working, individual Masons were questioned for passwords before a lodge could open in a new degree.
Originally, in Moderns lodges, Wardens sat in the West beside a pillar near the lodge entrance, guarding the 'gate'.
When the first and second degree ceremonies were created from the old Fellows degree, one pillar was attached to each ceremony.
In operative times, the Lodge of Fellows was the only kind of lodge.
source_notes: Commentary on the Second Degree | Passing the Junior Warden: The Gate of the Lodge | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\commentary_on_the_second_Degree.md#passing-the-junior-warden-the-gate-of-the-lodge | section 5

changes.full_summary_block:
### Enrichment from Commentary on the Second Degree
Source section: The Winding Staircase: Origins in the Temple

This section details the winding staircase as a key feature of the English Second Degree, tracing its origins to the Temple of Solomon where priests used it to access three-tiered chambers. It describes the ritualistic movement of the steps from North to East and connects the fifteen or more steps to an early Christian tale of Mary, the mother of Jesus, ascending a fifteen-step staircase to the Temple, an event also depicted in medieval Guild Plays.
practical_elements: The ascent of the winding staircase is a distinctive feature of the English second degree.
Priests accessed the staircase through the south side wall half-way along the Holy Place.
The steps demonstrated move from North to East.
The staircase consists of fifteen or more steps.
changes.symbolic_meaning: The winding staircase symbolizes a path for priests to move between the Holy Place and the Temple's chambers, representing access and progression within sacred spaces. Its connection to Mary's ascent to the Temple further imbues it with a sense of spiritual journey and welcome into sacred precincts.
tradition_notes: The winding staircase originated as a means for priests to pass between the Holy Place and the three-tiered chambers of the Temple of Solomon.
Some Second Degree tracing boards correctly show priests' access to this staircase through the south side wall.
The fifteen or more steps derive from an early Christian tale of Mary, the mother of Jesus, ascending a staircase of fifteen steps to enter the Temple court.
This event was portrayed in Guild Plays performed by medieval masons.
source_notes: Commentary on the Second Degree | The Winding Staircase: Origins in the Temple | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\commentary_on_the_second_Degree.md#the-winding-staircase-origins-in-the-temple | section 7

changes.full_summary_block:
### Enrichment from Commentary on the Second Degree
Source section: Preparation for the Obligation: Dress and the Square

This section details the preparation of the candidate for the obligation in the Second Degree, focusing on the specific attire and the symbolic use of the Square. It explains the historical context of the candidate's dress, noting that ancient Masonic practice for this degree resembled the current Third Degree attire, and connects it to the practical dress of working masons. The importance of the Square is highlighted through its physical placement with the candidate and its representation in the degree's signs.
practical_elements: The candidate's other arm and foot are bared, differing from the former degree.
The candidate's left arm rests in the angle of the Square.
Working masons historically rolled up their sleeves and wore knee-length pants.
changes.symbolic_meaning: The specific attire and the prominent placement of the Square during the obligation emphasize the practical and moral principles associated with the Fellow Craft degree. The Square, as a working tool, symbolizes rectitude, morality, and the proper conduct of life, reinforced by its presence in the degree's signs and the candidate's posture.
changes.candidate_lesson: The preparation, including the specific dress and interaction with the Square, teaches the candidate about the historical roots of Masonic practice and the enduring importance of the Square as a symbol of moral conduct and the duties expected in the Fellow Craft degree.
tradition_notes: Originally, there was only one grade of Mason in a Lodge: Fellow or Master of the Craft.
In ancient Masonry, the candidate was attired as they are now in the Third Degree.
Working masons historically rolled up their sleeves and wore pants that only came to the knee.
source_notes: Commentary on the Second Degree | Preparation for the Obligation: Dress and the Square | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\commentary_on_the_second_Degree.md#preparation-for-the-obligation-dress-and-the-square | section 8

changes.full_summary_block:
### Enrichment from Commentary on the Second Degree
Source section: Halving the Word and the Position of the Pillars

This section clarifies two aspects of Masonic ritual: the historical practice of 'halving the word' and the correct orientation of the two pillars. It notes that the method of halving the word can vary between jurisdictions, potentially causing confusion. Regarding the pillars, it explains that their description as 'looking East' differs in perspective between Solomon's Temple (looking out) and a Masonic lodge (looking in). It also highlights that the commemoration of an Assistant High Priest by the second pillar is found in the Pseudepigrapha, not the Volume of the Sacred Law.
practical_elements: The lettering or halving of a 'secret' word was a common practice in the 18th Century or before.
In some Masonic rituals, especially in the USA, the letters for the word are given haphazardly, unlike the order used in England.
The pillars are described as if looking East.
In Solomon's Temple, looking East meant looking out of the building, while in the lodge, it means looking in.
changes.symbolic_meaning: The distinction in pillar orientation between Solomon's Temple and the lodge (looking out vs. looking in) symbolically represents different perspectives or focuses within the Masonic context. The 'halving the word' practice is a ritualistic element, inherently symbolic of esoteric knowledge and its transmission.
changes.candidate_lesson: Candidates should be aware that ritualistic elements, such as the order of letters in a word, can vary between jurisdictions, which might be puzzling. Understanding the correct orientation of the pillars, distinguishing between their placement in Solomon's Temple and a lodge, is crucial for comprehending lodge symbolism.
tradition_notes: The practice of halving a 'secret' word was common in the 18th Century or earlier.
The status of the figure commemorated by the second pillar as Assistant High Priest is recorded in the Pseudepigrapha, not the Volume of the Sacred Law.
caution_notes: The haphazard order of letters in some Masonic rituals (e.g., USA) can puzzle the candidate, never mind a Cowan.
source_notes: Commentary on the Second Degree | Halving the Word and the Position of the Pillars | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\commentary_on_the_second_Degree.md#halving-the-word-and-the-position-of-the-pillars | section 11

changes.full_summary_block:
### Enrichment from Commentary on the Second Degree
Source section: The Two Pillars Conjoined

This section explains that the two pillars, traditionally associated with the Fellow Craft degree, were originally part of a single ceremony, as evidenced by early floor-cloths and the historical practice of conferring both Apprentice and Fellow degrees on the same evening, particularly at the Grand Lodge of All England at York until 1780. The ritualistic question posed by the Senior Warden further supports this conjoint meaning, which is linked to an embellished translation of 1 Kings, chapter 8, verse 13.
practical_elements: The Senior Warden asks, 'And what when conjoined with that in the former degree?'
Floor-cloths or early tracing boards show the pillars together.
changes.symbolic_meaning: The conjoint meaning of the two pillars is an embellished translation of 1 Kings, chapter 8, verse 13, indicating their intended unity and significance within the ritual.
tradition_notes: In the Grand Lodge of All England at York, it was customary up to 1780 to make men Apprentices and Fellows on the same evening.
A trace of this practice is still evident in the ritual through the Senior Warden's question.
source_notes: Commentary on the Second Degree | The Two Pillars Conjoined | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\commentary_on_the_second_Degree.md#the-two-pillars-conjoined | section 12

changes.full_summary_block:
### Enrichment from Commentary on the Second Degree
Source section: The Working Tools and Their Moral Application

This section introduces the working tools of a stonemason, which are also found on the seats of the Worshipful Master and Wardens. As Free & Accepted Masons, these tools are applied to morals, with a longer explanation teaching important lessons, including duty to God. The presentation concludes with the aspiration to ascend to 'immortal mansions whence all goodness emanates,' emphasizing God's presence and the requirement of 'Holiness to the Lord,' as declared by the seal of the United Grand Lodge.
practical_elements: Working tools of a stonemason
Tools adorn the seats of the Worshipful Master and Wardens
changes.symbolic_meaning: The working tools, typically used by stonemasons, are symbolically applied to morals by Free & Accepted Masons. The aspiration to 'ascend to those immortal mansions whence all goodness emanates' symbolizes a spiritual goal and connection to God. The phrase 'Holiness to the Lord' from the United Grand Lodge seal signifies the ultimate moral and spiritual standard required.
changes.candidate_lesson: Candidates are taught that the working tools of a stonemason have a moral application in Freemasonry, guiding them towards self-improvement and a deeper understanding of their duty to God. The lesson emphasizes the pursuit of holiness and goodness, with the ultimate goal of spiritual ascent.
tradition_notes: The practice of applying working tools to morals is a distinctive aspect of Free & Accepted Masonry, differentiating it from operative masonry.
The phrase 'Holiness to the Lord' is a declaration found on the seal of the United Grand Lodge.
source_notes: Commentary on the Second Degree | The Working Tools and Their Moral Application | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\commentary_on_the_second_Degree.md#the-working-tools-and-their-moral-application | section 16
